report.title: Anthropic ships Memory API for Claude
report.summary: Public API to give Claude persistent memory across conversations. Drop-in for agent-style apps that need state.

report.title: OpenAI Spring update: cheaper o-series, stronger tool use
report.summary: o-series price drops ~30% across the board. New strict-tool-call mode for agentic workflows.

report.title: DeepMind's AlphaLearning paper: subgoal scaffolding for LLMs
report.summary: Research showing 12-point improvement on long-context reasoning by structuring subgoals as a graph during inference.

report.title: llama.cpp 2.0 ships with full CUDA + Metal parity
report.summary: Major rewrite. Same binary now runs identically across NVIDIA, Apple Silicon, and CPU. Top trending repo this week.

report.title: Garry Tan: 'Vibe coding is now the default for solo founders'
report.summary: Lightcone Podcast episode arguing the median YC company in W26 is shipping 70%+ AI-generated code.

learn.name: llama.cpp 2.0
learn.oneLineDescription: High-performance C++ inference engine for running LLMs locally on your laptop.

detail.whatItDoes: Lets you run open-weight large language models (Llama, Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek) directly on your CPU, Apple Silicon, or NVIDIA GPU. Single C++ binary, no Python, no Docker, no cloud. Loads quantized GGUF model files and serves them via a REST API or interactive CLI.
detail.whoItsFor: Developers who want to run LLMs offline, prototype agents without API costs, fine-tune locally, or ship desktop apps with embedded AI. Builders allergic to cloud lock-in. Anyone whose laptop can spare 8-16GB of RAM.
pros: Truly local: zero API calls, full privacy, works offline
Cross-platform: same binary runs on Mac (Metal), Linux/Windows (CUDA), or pure CPU
Mature ecosystem: GGUF model format is the de-facto local-LLM standard
cons: Inference speed depends on hardware: an M1 MacBook is fine for 7B models, slower for 70B
GGUF model conversion can be a one-time pain if you start with a HuggingFace transformer checkpoint
detail.setupGuideMarkdown:
## Setup llama.cpp 2.0 on macOS (Apple Silicon)

```bash
# 1. Clone and build
git clone https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cpp
cd llama.cpp
make LLAMA_METAL=1

# 2. Download a quantized model (e.g., Llama 3 8B, ~5GB)
curl -L -o models/llama-3-8b-q4.gguf \
  https://huggingface.co/QuantFactory/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct-GGUF/resolve/main/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct.Q4_K_M.gguf

# 3. Run interactive chat
./main -m models/llama-3-8b-q4.gguf -p "Hello, world" -n 256
```

If the build complains about missing tools, install Xcode CLI: `xcode-select --install`. Reasonable laptops produce ~30 tokens/sec on Llama 3 8B Q4.

learn.name: Anthropic Memory API
learn.oneLineDescription: Persistent memory for Claude that survives across conversations.

detail.whatItDoes: Adds long-term memory to Claude. Conversations can write to a memory store keyed by user ID; subsequent conversations retrieve relevant memories automatically. The model decides when to write and when to read; you can also call the memory store directly via the API for explicit control.
detail.whoItsFor: Anyone building agent-style products: chatbots, personal assistants, customer-support bots, coding agents. If your app currently passes a giant 'previous turns' blob into every request, this replaces that with structured retrieval and dramatically lowers token cost.
pros: Drop-in: existing message-completion calls work, memory is opt-in via a single header
Persists across sessions per user — no more 'sorry, who are you?' on every login
Claude decides relevance, so the prompt stays clean even with thousands of memories
cons: Beta: schema and pricing are not yet final, expect breaking changes through Q2 2026
Per-user memory adds a privacy surface — you need a clear deletion path for GDPR/CCPA

learn.name: DSPy 3.0
learn.oneLineDescription: Compile-don't-prompt framework for building reliable LLM pipelines.

detail.whatItDoes: DSPy treats your LLM pipeline like compiled code: you declare signatures (inputs/outputs), provide a few examples, and DSPy optimizes the underlying prompts automatically. When you change models or improve the data, it re-compiles. No more hand-tuning prompts forever.
detail.whoItsFor: Engineers tired of prompt-engineering whack-a-mole. Teams running production LLM pipelines that need to be portable across models. Researchers benchmarking prompting strategies.
pros: Treats prompts as compilation targets — change the model, re-compile, ship
Built-in optimizers (BootstrapFewShot, MIPRO) consistently beat hand-tuned prompts
Stanford-NLP-backed, real research velocity, active community
cons: Steep concept curve: 'signatures', 'modules', 'optimizers' takes a weekend to internalize
Less useful for one-shot generation tasks where prompt-tuning is overkill
detail.setupGuideMarkdown:
## Setup DSPy in Python

```bash
pip install dspy-ai
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...
```

```python
import dspy

# Configure your LM
lm = dspy.LM("anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6")
dspy.configure(lm=lm)

# Declare a signature: inputs and outputs
class Summarize(dspy.Signature):
    """Summarize a paragraph in one sentence."""
    paragraph = dspy.InputField()
    summary = dspy.OutputField(desc="one-sentence summary")

# Use it like a function
summarize = dspy.ChainOfThought(Summarize)
result = summarize(paragraph="DSPy is a framework that lets you...")
print(result.summary)
```

For real pipelines, add `BootstrapFewShot` optimizer with a small training set (5-20 examples) and let DSPy auto-tune the prompt.
